posts.text: Bad actors gain in power and prominence -> polarization increases -> stakes grow -> imperative to own your enemies -> willingness to engage in bad faith arguments to defend your own side -> loss of trust in "independent" institutions and experts -> bad actors gain in power and prominence -> polarization increases  -> stakes grow -> imperative to own your enemies -> willingness to engage in bad faith arguments to defend your own side -> loss of trust in "independe

A wonderful way to finish a difficult year:

The Identity Trap was chosen as a Best Book of 2023 by publications including The Economist, The Financial Times, and Prospect Magazine.

If you want to understand the new ideology transforming our culture, please give it a look.

And no, the fact that Musk isn't a consistent defender of free speech does not imply that we shouldn't fight for a genuine culture of free speech—any more than the fact that many campaigners against corruption turn out to be deeply corrupt implies that you shouldn't get rid of corruption.

Shockingly, Elon Musk's commitment to free speech does not appear to be entirely consistent.

(On the bright side, I'm feeling more confident than ever in my new rule of thumb: Everyone in American public life eventually lives up to the most absurd caricature of themselves.)


Elon Musk (@elonmusk)

As I said earlier this week, “decolonization”, “from the river to the sea” and similar euphemisms necessarily imply genocide.

Clear calls for extreme violence are against our terms of service and wi

One day a few weeks ago, I did three interviews about The Identity Trap.

The first interviewer insisted that racially segregated affinity groups in schools are a figment of my imagination.

The other two both told me their children were divided into such groups in their schools


Razib Khan 🧬 ✍️ (@razibkhan)

my daughter's middle school has 'affinity groups.' about 20% of the day spent doing stuff in groups. divided by sex, sexuality and also jewish/not jewish and race. she picked the asian gro

The poster implying that Rishi Sunak is a "coconut" because he does not act in the ways that "brown" people "should" is both stupid and offensive.

But that just shows how dangerous it is to ban offensive speech. If robust criticism of a sitting Prime Minister is not allowed, then what is?

Eisen's firing is quickly becoming one of the canonical examples of this moment.

One thing this piece does is to give much more detail of how it came about—and to show the very interesting ways in which Eisen's own thoughts on "cancel culture" have evolved.

The firing of Mike Eisen as editor of eLife was wrong.

To understand why, you need to take a view of free speech that's consistent *and* broad—one that protects people even if their speech is controversial and their actions imperfect.

Me @TheAtlantic.
theatlantic.com/ideas/archiv…
(And with that, I'm going to take a little break from this platform. I may be back on occasion to post articles and other content. But don't expect me to be around to comment day-to-day for a while.)

The Identity Trap is back in stock! 

Please read this book. I've been reluctant to post about it in the past weeks, but I promise it will help you to understand the ideology that created this moment—and how we can push back against it in a principled, effective way.

It is a little on the nose that Pakistan is in the process of expelling 1.7 million (!) Afghans, many of whom will be violently persecuted by the Taliban, and nobody in the world seems to care one iota about their suffering.


Reuters World (@ReutersWorld)

Afghans return to Taliban rule as Pakistan moves to expel 1.7 million reut.rs/3FCpJJh

🙏🙏🙏


Caitlin Flanagan (@CaitlinPacific)

Or as @Yascha_Mounk calls it, "the short march"

His new book The Identity Trap is great



— https://nitter.net/CaitlinPacific/status/1719809584666472792#m

Hamas murdered over 1,400 people in an indescribably sadistic manner.

Its leaders have made it very clear that they will continue to murder Israeli civilians if given the chance.

Israel has every right to defend itself.

But that is all the more reason for Israel to operate in accordance with the rules of war. Its attacks must have military targets. And they must—even if Hamas uses human shields in a craven attempt to create bad publicity for Israel, as the terrorist organization undoubtedly d

Kidding apart, this is a lovely recognition for The Identity Trap! And the list puts me in the  company of many writers I greatly admire.

Thank you!

Yascha: "Siri, define 'back-handed compliment'."

Siri: "Your book was listed as a Best Book of 2023... by the 'non-obvious book awards.'"

I have a whole chapter on the policies and social norms we need to mount an energetic defense of a culture of free speech in ways that (unlike some of the recent pushback, e.g. in Florida) don’t themselves undermine it in The Identity Trap, by the way.

Your Favorite Publication Needs You. Enter our reader survey—and win a Persuasion t-shirt!
Medical Research Is Hopelessly Caught in Red Tape (by Ruxandra Teslo). To have AI breakthroughs in medicine, you first need to fix bureaucratic bottlenecks.

Adrian Wooldridge on the Lost Genius of the Political Center. Yascha Mounk and Adrian Wooldridge explore how liberalism reinvented itself through past crises—and what that means for its survival today.
How Trans Activism Became So Radical (by Jamie Paul). The trans movement won the lottery. Then they lost it all.
How Not To Do Regime Change (by Francis Fukuyama). Nearly two weeks in, it’s clear just how ill thought-through Trump’s regional war is.
The Bourgeoisie Has Switched Sides. What happens when elites all believe the same thing.
The End of “Legitimacy” (by Sam Kahn). The United States has forfeited its moral high ground.
What Regime Change Could Mean for Iran (by Saeid Golkar). The Islamic Republic enters a moment that could quickly become a legitimacy crisis.
A Very Brief Interview with Klaus Schwab. The founder of the World Economic Forum asked me for a blurb. I offered him an interview. 20 minutes in, he cut it short.
North Korea Was Right About Nuclear Weapons (by Shahn Louis)
Expecting Iran to Unconditionally Surrender Is a Fool’s Errand (by Francis Fukuyama)
Episode 182: Christopher Clark on A Scandal in Königsberg (by Richard Aldous). Bookstack at Persuasion is delivered to you every other Sunday at 6am EST.
Dean Ball on Who Should Control AI. Yascha Mounk and Dean Ball examine how the fight over autonomous weapons and mass surveillance reveals the impossible choices facing American AI policy.
This Week At Persuasion (by Leonora Barclay). The Book Club is back! Plus, Francis Fukuyama on Western Civilization.
Fracking Killed Khamenei (by Quico Toro)
